revision.text:
{{Infobox new religious movement | name = Oomoto Hikari no Michi | native_name = 大夲光之道 | native_name_lang = ja | logo = | classification = [[New religious movement]] | theology = [[Shinto]]-derived, [[Oomoto]] | founder = | founded_date = c. 1980s (Registered) | location = [[Toyooka, Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] | leader = | main_interest = World peace, Spiritual purification | website = {{URL|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}} }}
'''Oomoto Hikari no Michi''' (Japanese: {{lang|ja|大夲光之道}}), also known as '''Oomoto Hikari no Michi Church''', is a [[Japanese new religion]] and an independent offshoot of the [[Oomoto]] (Oomoto-kyo) tradition.<ref name="BunkaCho">{{cite report |title=Shūkyō Nenkan (Religion Yearbook) |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs, Government of Japan |year=2022 |url=https://www.bunka.go.jp/tokei_hakusho_shuppan/hakusho_nenjihokokusho/shukyo_nenkan/pdf/r04nenkan.pdf |page=162 |language=Japanese}}</ref> Headquartered in [[Toyooka, Hyōgo|Toyooka]], Hyōgo Prefecture, the group is distinct from the mainstream Oomoto organization based in [[Kameoka, Kyoto|Kameoka]] and [[Ayabe]], though it shares the same spiritual lineage and foundational scriptures.
== History == Oomoto Hikari no Michi was established as a separate religious juridical person (''shūkyō hōjin'') distinct from the main Oomoto foundation. The organization is listed in the official records of the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] of Japan, which categorizes it among Shinto-derived religious groups.<ref name="BunkaCho"/>
The group distinguishes itself by the specific use of the kanji character ''to'' ({{lang|ja|夲}}) in "Oomoto" ({{lang|ja|大夲}}), a variant of the character ''hon'' ({{lang|ja|本}}) commonly used by the mainstream Oomoto group ({{lang|ja|大本}}). This stylistic choice is often associated with the calligraphy and spiritual teachings of the co-founder [[Onisaburo Deguchi]], emphasizing an orthodox or esoteric interpretation of the movement's roots.<ref>{{cite web |title=Oomoto Hikari no Michi Official Website |url=https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/ |accessdate=2024-05-18}}</ref>
While the mainstream Oomoto organization underwent a highly publicized succession dispute in 2001 following the death of the Fourth Spiritual Leader [[Kiyoko Deguchi]] (which resulted in a schism between the followers of the Fifth Leader [[Kurenai Deguchi]] and the Oomoto-Shinto/Aizen-en faction),<ref>{{cite news |title=Oomoto dispute settled |publisher=The Japan Times |date=2006-07-06 |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2006/07/06/national/oomoto-dispute-settled/}}</ref> Oomoto Hikari no Michi is listed as having established its independent legal status prior to this specific event, appearing in government religious yearbooks with a founding registration date in the 1980s.<ref name="WikidataList">{{cite web |title=List of Religions |publisher=Wikidata |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Wikidata:List_of_religions}}</ref>
== Beliefs == As an offshoot of Oomoto, the organization's doctrine is rooted in the teachings of [[Nao Deguchi]] and [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]. Key tenets generally include: * The worship of ''Ushitora no Konjin'' (later identified with [[Kunitokotachi]]). * The practice of spiritual purification. * The pursuit of world peace and the unity of all religions (''Bankyo Dokon''). * The concept of "Hikari no Michi" (The Path of Light) as a method of living in accordance with divine will.
== See also == * [[Oomoto]] * [[Japanese new religions]] * [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}}
''Note: Generated by Gemini 3 Pro in December 2025''
[[Category:Oomoto]] [[Category:Japanese new religions]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 1980s]] [[Category:Organizations based in Hyogo Prefecture]]

{{Infobox organization | name = Oomoto Hikari no Michi | native_name = 大夲光之道 | native_name_lang = ja | logo = | classification = [[New religious movement]] | theology = [[Shinto]]-derived, [[Oomoto]] | founder = | foundation = c. 1980s (Registered) | location = [[Toyooka, Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] | leader = | main_interest = World peace, Spiritual purification | website = {{URL|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}} }}
'''Oomoto Hikari no Michi''' (Japanese: {{lang|ja|大夲光之道}}), also known as '''Oomoto Hikari no Michi Church''', is a [[Japanese new religion]] and an independent offshoot of the [[Oomoto]] (Oomoto-kyo) tradition.<ref name="BunkaCho">{{cite report |title=Shūkyō Nenkan (Religion Yearbook) |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs, Government of Japan |year=2022 |url=https://www.bunka.go.jp/tokei_hakusho_shuppan/hakusho_nenjihokokusho/shukyo_nenkan/pdf/r04nenkan.pdf |page=162 |language=Japanese}}</ref> Headquartered in [[Toyooka, Hyōgo|Toyooka]], Hyōgo Prefecture, the group is distinct from the mainstream Oomoto organization based in [[Kameoka, Kyoto|Kameoka]] and [[Ayabe]], though it shares the same spiritual lineage and foundational scriptures.
== History == Oomoto Hikari no Michi was established as a separate religious juridical person (''shūkyō hōjin'') distinct from the main Oomoto foundation. The organization is listed in the official records of the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] of Japan, which categorizes it among Shinto-derived religious groups.<ref name="BunkaCho"/>
The group distinguishes itself by the specific use of the kanji character ''to'' ({{lang|ja|夲}}) in "Oomoto" ({{lang|ja|大夲}}), a variant of the character ''hon'' ({{lang|ja|本}}) commonly used by the mainstream Oomoto group ({{lang|ja|大本}}). This stylistic choice is often associated with the calligraphy and spiritual teachings of the co-founder [[Onisaburo Deguchi]], emphasizing an orthodox or esoteric interpretation of the movement's roots.<ref>{{cite web |title=Oomoto Hikari no Michi Official Website |url=https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/ |accessdate=2024-05-18}}</ref>
While the mainstream Oomoto organization underwent a highly publicized succession dispute in 2001 following the death of the Fourth Spiritual Leader [[Kiyoko Deguchi]] (which resulted in a schism between the followers of the Fifth Leader [[Kurenai Deguchi]] and the Oomoto-Shinto/Aizen-en faction),<ref>{{cite news |title=Oomoto dispute settled |publisher=The Japan Times |date=2006-07-06 |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2006/07/06/national/oomoto-dispute-settled/}}</ref> Oomoto Hikari no Michi is listed as having established its independent legal status prior to this specific event, appearing in government religious yearbooks with a founding registration date in the 1980s.<ref name="WikidataList">{{cite web |title=List of Religions |publisher=Wikidata |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Wikidata:List_of_religions}}</ref>
== Beliefs == As an offshoot of Oomoto, the organization's doctrine is rooted in the teachings of [[Nao Deguchi]] and [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]. Key tenets generally include: * The worship of ''Ushitora no Konjin'' (later identified with [[Kunitokotachi]]). * The practice of spiritual purification. * The pursuit of world peace and the unity of all religions (''Bankyo Dokon''). * The concept of "Hikari no Michi" (The Path of Light) as a method of living in accordance with divine will.
== See also == * [[Oomoto]] * [[Japanese new religions]] * [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}}
''Note: Generated by Gemini 3 Pro in December 2025''
[[Category:Oomoto]] [[Category:Japanese new religions]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 1980s]] [[Category:Organizations based in Hyogo Prefecture]]

{{Infobox organization | name = Oomoto Hikari no Michi | native_name = 大夲光之道 | native_name_lang = ja | logo = | classification = [[New religious movement]] | theology = [[Shinto]]-derived, [[Oomoto]] | founder = | foundation = c. 1980s (Registered) | location = [[Toyooka, Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] | leader = | main_interest = World peace, Spiritual purification | website = {{URL|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}} }}
'''Oomoto Hikari no Michi''' (Japanese: {{lang|ja|大夲光之道}}), also known as '''Oomoto Hikari no Michi Church''', is a [[Japanese new religion]] and an independent offshoot of the [[Oomoto]] (Oomoto-kyo) tradition.<ref name="BunkaCho">{{cite report |title=Shūkyō Nenkan (Religion Yearbook) |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs, Government of Japan |year=2022 |url=https://www.bunka.go.jp/tokei_hakusho_shuppan/hakusho_nenjihokokusho/shukyo_nenkan/pdf/r04nenkan.pdf |page=162 |language=Japanese}}</ref> Headquartered in [[Toyooka, Hyōgo|Toyooka]], Hyōgo Prefecture, the group is distinct from the mainstream Oomoto organization based in [[Kameoka, Kyoto|Kameoka]] and [[Ayabe]], though it shares the same spiritual lineage and foundational scriptures.
== History == Oomoto Hikari no Michi was established as a separate religious juridical person (''shūkyō hōjin'') distinct from the main Oomoto foundation. The organization is listed in the official records of the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] of Japan, which categorizes it among Shinto-derived religious groups.<ref name="BunkaCho"/>
The group distinguishes itself by the specific use of the kanji character ''to'' ({{lang|ja|夲}}) in "Oomoto" ({{lang|ja|大夲}}), a variant of the character ''hon'' ({{lang|ja|本}}) commonly used by the mainstream Oomoto group ({{lang|ja|大本}}). This stylistic choice is often associated with the calligraphy and spiritual teachings of the co-founder [[Onisaburo Deguchi]], emphasizing an orthodox or esoteric interpretation of the movement's roots.<ref>{{cite web |title=Oomoto Hikari no Michi Official Website |url=https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/ |accessdate=2024-05-18}}</ref>
While the mainstream Oomoto organization underwent a highly publicized succession dispute in 2001 following the death of the Fourth Spiritual Leader [[Kiyoko Deguchi]] (which resulted in a schism between the followers of the Fifth Leader [[Kurenai Deguchi]] and the Oomoto-Shinto/Aizen-en faction),<ref>{{cite news |title=Oomoto dispute settled |publisher=The Japan Times |date=2006-07-06 |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2006/07/06/national/oomoto-dispute-settled/}}</ref> Oomoto Hikari no Michi is listed as having established its independent legal status prior to this specific event, appearing in government religious yearbooks with a founding registration date in the 1980s.<ref name="WikidataList">{{cite web |title=List of Religions |publisher=Wikidata |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Wikidata:List_of_religions}}</ref>
== Beliefs == As an offshoot of Oomoto, the organization's doctrine is rooted in the teachings of [[Nao Deguchi]] and [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]. Key tenets generally include: * The worship of ''Ushitora no Konjin'' (later identified with [[Kunitokotachi]]). * The practice of spiritual purification. * The pursuit of world peace and the unity of all religions (''Bankyo Dokon''). * The concept of "Hikari no Michi" (The Path of Light) as a method of living in accordance with divine will.
== See also == * [[Oomoto]] * [[Japanese new religions]] * [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}}
''Note: Generated by Gemini 3 Pro in December 2025''
[[Category:Oomoto]] [[Category:Japanese new religions]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 1980s]] [[Category:Organizations based in Hyogo Prefecture]][[Category:orphaned pages]]

{{Infobox organization | name = Oomoto Hikari no Michi | native_name = 大夲光之道 | native_name_lang = ja | logo = | classification = [[New religious movement]] | theology = [[Shinto]]-derived, [[Oomoto]] | founder = | foundation = c. 1980s (Registered) | location = [[Toyooka, Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] | leader = | main_interest = World peace, Spiritual purification | website = {{URL|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}} }}
'''Oomoto Hikari no Michi''' (Japanese: {{lang|ja|大夲光之道}}), also known as '''Oomoto Hikari no Michi Church''', is a [[Japanese new religion]] and an independent offshoot of the [[Oomoto]] (Oomoto-kyo) tradition.<ref name="BunkaCho">{{cite report |title=Shūkyō Nenkan (Religion Yearbook) |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs, Government of Japan |year=2022 |url=https://www.bunka.go.jp/tokei_hakusho_shuppan/hakusho_nenjihokokusho/shukyo_nenkan/pdf/r04nenkan.pdf |page=162 |language=Japanese}}</ref> Headquartered in [[Toyooka, Hyōgo|Toyooka]], Hyōgo Prefecture, the group is distinct from the mainstream Oomoto organization based in [[Kameoka, Kyoto|Kameoka]] and [[Ayabe]], though it shares the same spiritual lineage and foundational scriptures.
== History == Oomoto Hikari no Michi was established as a separate religious juridical person (''shūkyō hōjin'') distinct from the main Oomoto foundation. The organization is listed in the official records of the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] of Japan, which categorizes it among Shinto-derived religious groups.<ref name="BunkaCho"/>
The group distinguishes itself by the specific use of the kanji character ''to'' ({{lang|ja|夲}}) in "Oomoto" ({{lang|ja|大夲}}), a variant of the character ''hon'' ({{lang|ja|本}}) commonly used by the mainstream Oomoto group ({{lang|ja|大本}}). This stylistic choice is often associated with the calligraphy and spiritual teachings of the co-founder [[Onisaburo Deguchi]], emphasizing an orthodox or esoteric interpretation of the movement's roots.<ref>{{cite web |title=Oomoto Hikari no Michi Official Website |url=https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/ |accessdate=2024-05-18}}</ref>
While the mainstream Oomoto organization underwent a highly publicized succession dispute in 2001 following the death of the Fourth Spiritual Leader [[Kiyoko Deguchi]] (which resulted in a schism between the followers of the Fifth Leader [[Kurenai Deguchi]] and the Oomoto-Shinto/Aizen-en faction),<ref>{{cite news |title=Oomoto dispute settled |publisher=The Japan Times |date=2006-07-06 |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2006/07/06/national/oomoto-dispute-settled/}}</ref> Oomoto Hikari no Michi is listed as having established its independent legal status prior to this specific event, appearing in government religious yearbooks with a founding registration date in the 1980s.<ref name="WikidataList">{{cite web |title=List of Religions |publisher=Wikidata |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Wikidata:List_of_religions}}</ref>
== Beliefs == As an offshoot of Oomoto, the organization's doctrine is rooted in the teachings of [[Nao Deguchi]] and [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]. Key tenets generally include: * The worship of ''Ushitora no Konjin'' (later identified with [[Kunitokotachi]]). * The practice of spiritual purification. * The pursuit of world peace and the unity of all religions (''Bankyo Dokon''). * The concept of "Hikari no Michi" (The Path of Light) as a method of living in accordance with divine will.
== See also == * [[Oomoto]] * [[Japanese new religions]] * [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}}
''Note: Generated by Gemini 3 Pro in December 2025''

{{Infobox organization | name = Oomoto Hikari no Michi | native_name = 大夲光之道 | native_name_lang = ja | logo = | classification = [[New religious movement]] | theology = [[Shinto]]-derived, [[Oomoto]] | founder = | foundation = c. 1980s (Registered) | location = [[Toyooka, Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] | leader = | main_interest = World peace, Spiritual purification | website = {{URL|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}} }}
'''Oomoto Hikari no Michi''' (Japanese: {{lang|ja|大夲光之道}}), also known as '''Oomoto Hikari no Michi Church''', is a [[Japanese new religion]] and an independent offshoot of the [[Oomoto]] (Oomoto-kyo) tradition.<ref name="BunkaCho">{{cite report |title=Shūkyō Nenkan (Religion Yearbook) |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs, Government of Japan |year=2022 |url=https://www.bunka.go.jp/tokei_hakusho_shuppan/hakusho_nenjihokokusho/shukyo_nenkan/pdf/r04nenkan.pdf |page=162 |language=Japanese}}</ref> Headquartered in [[Toyooka, Hyōgo|Toyooka]], Hyōgo Prefecture, the group is distinct from the mainstream Oomoto organization based in [[Kameoka, Kyoto|Kameoka]] and [[Ayabe]], though it shares the same spiritual lineage and foundational scriptures.
== History == Oomoto Hikari no Michi was established as a separate religious juridical person (''shūkyō hōjin'') distinct from the main Oomoto foundation. The organization is listed in the official records of the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] of Japan, which categorizes it among Shinto-derived religious groups.<ref name="BunkaCho"/>
The group distinguishes itself by the specific use of the kanji character ''to'' ({{lang|ja|夲}}) in "Oomoto" ({{lang|ja|大夲}}), a variant of the character ''hon'' ({{lang|ja|本}}) commonly used by the mainstream Oomoto group ({{lang|ja|大本}}). This stylistic choice is often associated with the calligraphy and spiritual teachings of the co-founder [[Onisaburo Deguchi]], emphasizing an orthodox or esoteric interpretation of the movement's roots.<ref>{{cite web |title=Oomoto Hikari no Michi Official Website |url=https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/ |accessdate=2024-05-18}}</ref>
While the mainstream Oomoto organization underwent a highly publicized succession dispute in 2001 following the death of the Fourth Spiritual Leader [[Kiyoko Deguchi]] (which resulted in a schism between the followers of the Fifth Leader [[Kurenai Deguchi]] and the Oomoto-Shinto/Aizen-en faction),<ref>{{cite news |title=Oomoto dispute settled |publisher=The Japan Times |date=2006-07-06 |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2006/07/06/national/oomoto-dispute-settled/}}</ref> Oomoto Hikari no Michi is listed as having established its independent legal status prior to this specific event, appearing in government religious yearbooks with a founding registration date in the 1980s.<ref name="WikidataList">{{cite web |title=List of Religions |publisher=Wikidata |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Wikidata:List_of_religions}}</ref>
== Beliefs == As an offshoot of Oomoto, the organization's doctrine is rooted in the teachings of [[Nao Deguchi]] and [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]. Key tenets generally include: * The worship of ''Ushitora no Konjin'' (later identified with [[Kunitokotachi]]). * The practice of spiritual purification. * The pursuit of world peace and the unity of all religions (''Bankyo Dokon''). * The concept of "Hikari no Michi" (The Path of Light) as a method of living in accordance with divine will.
== See also == * [[Oomoto]] * [[Japanese new religions]] * [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}}
''Note: Generated by Gemini 3 Pro in December 2025''
[[Category:Oomoto]] [[Category:Japanese new religions]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 1980s]] [[Category:Organizations based in Hyogo Prefecture]][[Category:orphaned pages]][[Category:orphaned pages]][[Category:orphaned pages]]

{{Infobox organization | name = Oomoto Hikari no Michi | native_name = 大夲光之道 | native_name_lang = ja | logo = | classification = [[New religious movement]] | theology = [[Shinto]]-derived, [[Oomoto]] | founder = | foundation = c. 1980s (Registered) | location = [[Toyooka, Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] | leader = | main_interest = World peace, Spiritual purification | website = {{URL|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}} }}
'''Oomoto Hikari no Michi''' (Japanese: {{lang|ja|大夲光之道}}), also known as '''Oomoto Hikari no Michi Church''', is a [[Japanese new religion]] and an independent offshoot of the [[Oomoto]] (Oomoto-kyo) tradition.<ref name="BunkaCho">{{cite report |title=Shūkyō Nenkan (Religion Yearbook) |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs, Government of Japan |year=2022 |url=https://www.bunka.go.jp/tokei_hakusho_shuppan/hakusho_nenjihokokusho/shukyo_nenkan/pdf/r04nenkan.pdf |page=162 |language=Japanese}}</ref> Headquartered in [[Toyooka, Hyōgo|Toyooka]], Hyōgo Prefecture, the group is distinct from the mainstream Oomoto organization based in [[Kameoka, Kyoto|Kameoka]] and [[Ayabe]], though it shares the same spiritual lineage and foundational scriptures.
== History == Oomoto Hikari no Michi was established as a separate religious juridical person (''shūkyō hōjin'') distinct from the main Oomoto foundation. The organization is listed in the official records of the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] of Japan, which categorizes it among Shinto-derived religious groups.<ref name="BunkaCho"/>
The group distinguishes itself by the specific use of the kanji character ''to'' ({{lang|ja|夲}}) in "Oomoto" ({{lang|ja|大夲}}), a variant of the character ''hon'' ({{lang|ja|本}}) commonly used by the mainstream Oomoto group ({{lang|ja|大本}}). This stylistic choice is often associated with the calligraphy and spiritual teachings of the co-founder [[Onisaburo Deguchi]], emphasizing an orthodox or esoteric interpretation of the movement's roots.<ref>{{cite web |title=Oomoto Hikari no Michi Official Website |url=https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/ |accessdate=2024-05-18}}</ref>
While the mainstream Oomoto organization underwent a highly publicized succession dispute in 2001 following the death of the Fourth Spiritual Leader [[Kiyoko Deguchi]] (which resulted in a schism between the followers of the Fifth Leader [[Kurenai Deguchi]] and the Oomoto-Shinto/Aizen-en faction),<ref>{{cite news |title=Oomoto dispute settled |publisher=The Japan Times |date=2006-07-06 |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2006/07/06/national/oomoto-dispute-settled/}}</ref> Oomoto Hikari no Michi is listed as having established its independent legal status prior to this specific event, appearing in government religious yearbooks with a founding registration date in the 1980s.<ref name="WikidataList">{{cite web |title=List of Religions |publisher=Wikidata |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Wikidata:List_of_religions}}</ref>
== Beliefs == As an offshoot of Oomoto, the organization's doctrine is rooted in the teachings of [[Nao Deguchi]] and [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]. Key tenets generally include: * The worship of ''Ushitora no Konjin'' (later identified with [[Kunitokotachi]]). * The practice of spiritual purification. * The pursuit of world peace and the unity of all religions (''Bankyo Dokon''). * The concept of "Hikari no Michi" (The Path of Light) as a method of living in accordance with divine will.
== See also == * [[Oomoto]] * [[Japanese new religions]] * [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}}
''Note: Generated by Gemini 3 Pro in December 2025''
[[Category:Oomoto]] [[Category:Japanese new religions]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 1980s]] [[Category:Organizations based in Hyogo Prefecture]][[Category:orphaned pages]][[Category:orphaned pages]][[Category:orphaned pages]][[Category:orphaned pages]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Orphaned pages]] to [[Category:Need translation]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox organization | name = Oomoto Hikari no Michi | native_name = 大夲光之道 | native_name_lang = ja | logo = | classification = [[New religious movement]] | theology = [[Shinto]]-derived, [[Oomoto]] | founder = | foundation = c. 1980s (Registered) | location = [[Toyooka, Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] | leader = | main_interest = World peace, Spiritual purification | website = {{URL|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}} }}
'''Oomoto Hikari no Michi''' (Japanese: {{lang|ja|大夲光之道}}), also known as '''Oomoto Hikari no Michi Church''', is a [[Japanese new religion]] and an independent offshoot of the [[Oomoto]] (Oomoto-kyo) tradition.<ref name="BunkaCho">{{cite report |title=Shūkyō Nenkan (Religion Yearbook) |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs, Government of Japan |year=2022 |url=https://www.bunka.go.jp/tokei_hakusho_shuppan/hakusho_nenjihokokusho/shukyo_nenkan/pdf/r04nenkan.pdf |page=162 |language=Japanese}}</ref> Headquartered in [[Toyooka, Hyōgo|Toyooka]], Hyōgo Prefecture, the group is distinct from the mainstream Oomoto organization based in [[Kameoka, Kyoto|Kameoka]] and [[Ayabe]], though it shares the same spiritual lineage and foundational scriptures.
== History == Oomoto Hikari no Michi was established as a separate religious juridical person (''shūkyō hōjin'') distinct from the main Oomoto foundation. The organization is listed in the official records of the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] of Japan, which categorizes it among Shinto-derived religious groups.<ref name="BunkaCho"/>
The group distinguishes itself by the specific use of the kanji character ''to'' ({{lang|ja|夲}}) in "Oomoto" ({{lang|ja|大夲}}), a variant of the character ''hon'' ({{lang|ja|本}}) commonly used by the mainstream Oomoto group ({{lang|ja|大本}}). This stylistic choice is often associated with the calligraphy and spiritual teachings of the co-founder [[Onisaburo Deguchi]], emphasizing an orthodox or esoteric interpretation of the movement's roots.<ref>{{cite web |title=Oomoto Hikari no Michi Official Website |url=https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/ |accessdate=2024-05-18}}</ref>
While the mainstream Oomoto organization underwent a highly publicized succession dispute in 2001 following the death of the Fourth Spiritual Leader [[Kiyoko Deguchi]] (which resulted in a schism between the followers of the Fifth Leader [[Kurenai Deguchi]] and the Oomoto-Shinto/Aizen-en faction),<ref>{{cite news |title=Oomoto dispute settled |publisher=The Japan Times |date=2006-07-06 |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2006/07/06/national/oomoto-dispute-settled/}}</ref> Oomoto Hikari no Michi is listed as having established its independent legal status prior to this specific event, appearing in government religious yearbooks with a founding registration date in the 1980s.<ref name="WikidataList">{{cite web |title=List of Religions |publisher=Wikidata |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Wikidata:List_of_religions}}</ref>
== Beliefs == As an offshoot of Oomoto, the organization's doctrine is rooted in the teachings of [[Nao Deguchi]] and [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]. Key tenets generally include: * The worship of ''Ushitora no Konjin'' (later identified with [[Kunitokotachi]]). * The practice of spiritual purification. * The pursuit of world peace and the unity of all religions (''Bankyo Dokon''). * The concept of "Hikari no Michi" (The Path of Light) as a method of living in accordance with divine will.
== See also == * [[Oomoto]] * [[Japanese new religions]] * [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}}
''Note: Generated by Gemini 3 Pro in December 2025''

revision.comment: Bot: tag page without wikidata link [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23128882600|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox organization | name = Oomoto Hikari no Michi | native_name = 大夲光之道 | native_name_lang = ja | logo = | classification = [[New religious movement]] | theology = [[Shinto]]-derived, [[Oomoto]] | founder = | foundation = c. 1980s (Registered) | location = [[Toyooka, Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] | leader = | main_interest = World peace, Spiritual purification | website = {{URL|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}} }}
'''Oomoto Hikari no Michi''' (Japanese: {{lang|ja|大夲光之道}}), also known as '''Oomoto Hikari no Michi Church''', is a [[Japanese new religion]] and an independent offshoot of the [[Oomoto]] (Oomoto-kyo) tradition.<ref name="BunkaCho">{{cite report |title=Shūkyō Nenkan (Religion Yearbook) |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs, Government of Japan |year=2022 |url=https://www.bunka.go.jp/tokei_hakusho_shuppan/hakusho_nenjihokokusho/shukyo_nenkan/pdf/r04nenkan.pdf |page=162 |language=Japanese}}</ref> Headquartered in [[Toyooka, Hyōgo|Toyooka]], Hyōgo Prefecture, the group is distinct from the mainstream Oomoto organization based in [[Kameoka, Kyoto|Kameoka]] and [[Ayabe]], though it shares the same spiritual lineage and foundational scriptures.
== History == Oomoto Hikari no Michi was established as a separate religious juridical person (''shūkyō hōjin'') distinct from the main Oomoto foundation. The organization is listed in the official records of the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] of Japan, which categorizes it among Shinto-derived religious groups.<ref name="BunkaCho"/>
The group distinguishes itself by the specific use of the kanji character ''to'' ({{lang|ja|夲}}) in "Oomoto" ({{lang|ja|大夲}}), a variant of the character ''hon'' ({{lang|ja|本}}) commonly used by the mainstream Oomoto group ({{lang|ja|大本}}). This stylistic choice is often associated with the calligraphy and spiritual teachings of the co-founder [[Onisaburo Deguchi]], emphasizing an orthodox or esoteric interpretation of the movement's roots.<ref>{{cite web |title=Oomoto Hikari no Michi Official Website |url=https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/ |accessdate=2024-05-18}}</ref>
While the mainstream Oomoto organization underwent a highly publicized succession dispute in 2001 following the death of the Fourth Spiritual Leader [[Kiyoko Deguchi]] (which resulted in a schism between the followers of the Fifth Leader [[Kurenai Deguchi]] and the Oomoto-Shinto/Aizen-en faction),<ref>{{cite news |title=Oomoto dispute settled |publisher=The Japan Times |date=2006-07-06 |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2006/07/06/national/oomoto-dispute-settled/}}</ref> Oomoto Hikari no Michi is listed as having established its independent legal status prior to this specific event, appearing in government religious yearbooks with a founding registration date in the 1980s.<ref name="WikidataList">{{cite web |title=List of Religions |publisher=Wikidata |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Wikidata:List_of_religions}}</ref>
== Beliefs == As an offshoot of Oomoto, the organization's doctrine is rooted in the teachings of [[Nao Deguchi]] and [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]. Key tenets generally include: * The worship of ''Ushitora no Konjin'' (later identified with [[Kunitokotachi]]). * The practice of spiritual purification. * The pursuit of world peace and the unity of all religions (''Bankyo Dokon''). * The concept of "Hikari no Michi" (The Path of Light) as a method of living in accordance with divine will.
== See also == * [[Oomoto]] * [[Japanese new religions]] * [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}}
''Note: Generated by Gemini 3 Pro in December 2025''
[[Category:Oomoto]] [[Category:Japanese new religions]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 1980s]] [[Category:Organizations based in Hyogo Prefecture]][[Category:Orphaned pages]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Orphaned pages]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Orphaned pages]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Orphaned pages]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Pages without wikidata]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Orphaned pages]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23392883474|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox organization | name = Oomoto Hikari no Michi | native_name = 大夲光之道 | native_name_lang = ja | logo = | classification = [[New religious movement]] | theology = [[Shinto]]-derived, [[Oomoto]] | founder = | foundation = c. 1980s (Registered) | location = [[Toyooka, Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] | leader = | main_interest = World peace, Spiritual purification | website = {{URL|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}} }}
'''Oomoto Hikari no Michi''' (Japanese: {{lang|ja|大夲光之道}}), also known as '''Oomoto Hikari no Michi Church''', is a [[Japanese new religion]] and an independent offshoot of the [[Oomoto]] (Oomoto-kyo) tradition.<ref name="BunkaCho">{{cite report |title=Shūkyō Nenkan (Religion Yearbook) |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs, Government of Japan |year=2022 |url=https://www.bunka.go.jp/tokei_hakusho_shuppan/hakusho_nenjihokokusho/shukyo_nenkan/pdf/r04nenkan.pdf |page=162 |language=Japanese}}</ref> Headquartered in [[Toyooka, Hyōgo|Toyooka]], Hyōgo Prefecture, the group is distinct from the mainstream Oomoto organization based in [[Kameoka, Kyoto|Kameoka]] and [[Ayabe]], though it shares the same spiritual lineage and foundational scriptures.
== History == Oomoto Hikari no Michi was established as a separate religious juridical person (''shūkyō hōjin'') distinct from the main Oomoto foundation. The organization is listed in the official records of the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] of Japan, which categorizes it among Shinto-derived religious groups.<ref name="BunkaCho"/>
The group distinguishes itself by the specific use of the kanji character ''to'' ({{lang|ja|夲}}) in "Oomoto" ({{lang|ja|大夲}}), a variant of the character ''hon'' ({{lang|ja|本}}) commonly used by the mainstream Oomoto group ({{lang|ja|大本}}). This stylistic choice is often associated with the calligraphy and spiritual teachings of the co-founder [[Onisaburo Deguchi]], emphasizing an orthodox or esoteric interpretation of the movement's roots.<ref>{{cite web |title=Oomoto Hikari no Michi Official Website |url=https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/ |accessdate=2024-05-18}}</ref>
While the mainstream Oomoto organization underwent a highly publicized succession dispute in 2001 following the death of the Fourth Spiritual Leader [[Kiyoko Deguchi]] (which resulted in a schism between the followers of the Fifth Leader [[Kurenai Deguchi]] and the Oomoto-Shinto/Aizen-en faction),<ref>{{cite news |title=Oomoto dispute settled |publisher=The Japan Times |date=2006-07-06 |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2006/07/06/national/oomoto-dispute-settled/}}</ref> Oomoto Hikari no Michi is listed as having established its independent legal status prior to this specific event, appearing in government religious yearbooks with a founding registration date in the 1980s.<ref name="WikidataList">{{cite web |title=List of Religions |publisher=Wikidata |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Wikidata:List_of_religions}}</ref>
== Beliefs == As an offshoot of Oomoto, the organization's doctrine is rooted in the teachings of [[Nao Deguchi]] and [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]. Key tenets generally include: * The worship of ''Ushitora no Konjin'' (later identified with [[Kunitokotachi]]). * The practice of spiritual purification. * The pursuit of world peace and the unity of all religions (''Bankyo Dokon''). * The concept of "Hikari no Michi" (The Path of Light) as a method of living in accordance with divine will.
== See also == * [[Oomoto]] * [[Japanese new religions]] * [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}}
''Note: Generated by Gemini 3 Pro in December 2025''
[[Category:Oomoto]] [[Category:Japanese new religions]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 1980s]] [[Category:Organizations based in Hyogo Prefecture]][[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Pages without wikidata]]

revision.comment: Sync from repo need_translation/ [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24618454543|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox organization | name = Oomoto Hikari no Michi | native_name = 大夲光之道 | native_name_lang = ja | logo = | classification = [[New religious movement]] | theology = [[Shinto]]-derived, [[Oomoto]] | founder = | foundation = c. 1980s (Registered) | location = [[Toyooka, Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] | leader = | main_interest = World peace, Spiritual purification | website = {{URL|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}} }}
'''Oomoto Hikari no Michi''' (Japanese: {{lang|ja|大夲光之道}}), also known as '''Oomoto Hikari no Michi Church''', is a [[Japanese new religion]] and an independent offshoot of the [[Oomoto]] (Oomoto-kyo) tradition.<ref name="BunkaCho">{{cite report |title=Shūkyō Nenkan (Religion Yearbook) |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs, Government of Japan |year=2022 |url=https://www.bunka.go.jp/tokei_hakusho_shuppan/hakusho_nenjihokokusho/shukyo_nenkan/pdf/r04nenkan.pdf |page=162 |language=Japanese}}</ref> Headquartered in [[Toyooka, Hyōgo|Toyooka]], Hyōgo Prefecture, the group is distinct from the mainstream Oomoto organization based in [[Kameoka, Kyoto|Kameoka]] and [[Ayabe]], though it shares the same spiritual lineage and foundational scriptures.
== History == Oomoto Hikari no Michi was established as a separate religious juridical person (''shūkyō hōjin'') distinct from the main Oomoto foundation. The organization is listed in the official records of the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] of Japan, which categorizes it among Shinto-derived religious groups.<ref name="BunkaCho"/>
The group distinguishes itself by the specific use of the kanji character ''to'' ({{lang|ja|夲}}) in "Oomoto" ({{lang|ja|大夲}}), a variant of the character ''hon'' ({{lang|ja|本}}) commonly used by the mainstream Oomoto group ({{lang|ja|大本}}). This stylistic choice is often associated with the calligraphy and spiritual teachings of the co-founder [[Onisaburo Deguchi]], emphasizing an orthodox or esoteric interpretation of the movement's roots.<ref>{{cite web |title=Oomoto Hikari no Michi Official Website |url=https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/ |accessdate=2024-05-18}}</ref>
While the mainstream Oomoto organization underwent a highly publicized succession dispute in 2001 following the death of the Fourth Spiritual Leader [[Kiyoko Deguchi]] (which resulted in a schism between the followers of the Fifth Leader [[Kurenai Deguchi]] and the Oomoto-Shinto/Aizen-en faction),<ref>{{cite news |title=Oomoto dispute settled |publisher=The Japan Times |date=2006-07-06 |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2006/07/06/national/oomoto-dispute-settled/}}</ref> Oomoto Hikari no Michi is listed as having established its independent legal status prior to this specific event, appearing in government religious yearbooks with a founding registration date in the 1980s.<ref name="WikidataList">{{cite web |title=List of Religions |publisher=Wikidata |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Wikidata:List_of_religions}}</ref>
== Beliefs == As an offshoot of Oomoto, the organization's doctrine is rooted in the teachings of [[Nao Deguchi]] and [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]. Key tenets generally include: * The worship of ''Ushitora no Konjin'' (later identified with [[Kunitokotachi]]). * The practice of spiritual purification. * The pursuit of world peace and the unity of all religions (''Bankyo Dokon''). * The concept of "Hikari no Michi" (The Path of Light) as a method of living in accordance with divine will.
== See also == * [[Oomoto]] * [[Japanese new religions]] * [[Onisaburo Deguchi]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://www.oomotohikarinomichi.jp/}}
